chunks.text: Come on. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Mm-hmm. If
Two weeks.
Welcome.

Thank you all for coming. I appreciate that. Welcome, Mr. Speaker and David and Raf. It's wonderful to have you this afternoon. Have you got some opening remarks that you want to make? And what- Not too many. Not too many. I'll hand to you then. Just very interested in the lines of questioning people might have for both of the services. I think the big change from last year is the passing of the Parliament Act- Mm-hmm... which shifts a whole lot of responsibilities back into Parliament itself, and states pretty clearly about what the purpose of the place is

and how it should work. There will be adjustments that are required in that. If you look at the Office of the Clerk, one of the things that's emerged, very quickly this year is the capacity for very significant numbers of amendments to be put on the table in the committee stage of the House.

But also much earlier in the calendar year, we saw the extraordinary number of submissions that came in and continues to come in for bills that are before the Parliament. And I think it's a reasonable assessment that the capacity of AI to produce lots of material from simple questions is going to be a challenge for us all as parliamentarians moving forward. But particularly at the moment, the Office of the Clerk, where they've got to have the time to be able to assess those amendments because you can't get away from the

right of members of Parliament to act like members of Parliament, when it comes to dealing in the legislative process. With the other thing that's been occupying a lot of time for the parliamentary service is the, I suppose, heightened awareness of security issues for both the personal circumstances of members of Parliament, but also for staff in elected offices.

And as we go into the 2026 election, a greater awareness too that some of the more interesting sort of emails that get sent to MPs could be acted on, and if they were, that would be pretty disastrous. So, a broader consideration of how they might support MPs, or MPs might be supported during that period of time.

Mm-hmm. As for my own office, it continues to operate on a fairly slim basis and which is unusual for me to say that, but there you go. It's seeing some changes with role and title retirement, and that will bring in a temporary regime up until the election, after which time both the gentlemen to my left and right will consult with whoever might be Speaker about where to from there.

But I hope that it continues to be open to all MPs as it currently is. Mm-hmm. That's all I wanted to say. Right. Thank you. And we'll open up for questions. And we'll start with Glen. Kia ora. Thank you. And I think I'll just continue on what you've talked about around submissions, papers on the table, um- Bills going through Parliament. And I guess for both the Office of the Clerk and Parliamentary Service, my first question is just around the money you have going forward. Are you going to be able to meet the expectations of what's coming? And I looked at, I think it was in April, there'd

been almost 650,000 submissions this term, which was a 582% rise in two parliaments in terms of submissions coming through this place. But then there's been a huge uptake in terms of 24% increase in legislation coming to select committee, et cetera.

So the funding you have now, that going forward, is that enough to sort of manage what is to come? And also the second part to that is, are you looking at projections or can you, in terms of what is coming in the next parliamentary term in terms of submissions and bills and amendment papers, et cetera? Yeah. So, I think you're certainly right to identify that big increase, and that number of submissions you mentioned is half of all submissions received in the last 30 years, come in this term of Parliament.

So it is a big uplift. Some of that's related to bills that are contentious, but the number of bills routinely getting more than 1,000 submissions each has increased greatly as well. So it is a general trend. We've had a significant uplift in funding, and that was based on what we think we need in order to support the volume of parliamentary work that we have now and we're likely to have in the future.

We can't be really precise about it, though, because really, our work is driven by decisions that you make, as members and as a parliament and the government make. So yes, we think so. But, we don't have any control over the volume or any real levers to pull other than to do the work. What we want to be able to do, and one of the things we've received some funding for in terms of investing in technology, is to help find more efficient ways to deal with that volume of work, really so that we can support you better and more quickly to enable you to do your jobs, particularly in getting

submissions from the public to select committees as quickly as possible, while still making sure they have the necessary scrutiny for those who are out of order, defamatory or things like that. So, yeah. I guess that's the long answer, and the short one is, yes, I think so. In terms of, obviously, accessibility to Parliament, it's a wonderful thing.

But it also creates challenges like capacity and the ease of submitting. And of course, we bring in AI and form submissions that can be copied and pasted. Mm. So again, I guess in terms of the funding going forward, is your office looking at any ways that can manage that better or ways of engagement with our communities where it isn't just...

Because often we think the volume of submissions that isn't necessarily create the outcome of what we're looking for. So is your office looking in some of those areas? Yeah. And a few committees have tried out alternative forms of engagement, surveying people, for example, and aiming to reach communities that don't participate in the process as often. And I think there's some value in that work.

The other thing that we've been looking at and talking with the Standing Orders Committee about is whether there are alternative ways for people to indicate their views about bills. Quite a lot of submissions, and it's a very large number, but if all they're saying is, "I do support this bill," or, "I don't support it," writing that down and submitting it through the process is quite a lot of work for everyone concerned. Whereas there may be another way they can simply indicate support or not supporting a bill that could also reach the select committee.

Mm-hmm. So we're talking about options for things like that as well. And that might mean that we get a smaller total number of submissions that are all substantive and people who just want to indicate support or not supporting a bill may have another channel. Mm. Which might make things a bit more manageable. You're careful not to go to some sort of de facto citizens referendum on every bill.

Yeah. Because not everything that is going to be considered in a select committee process is available for all submitters to consider. It's why we have select committees. So there's a- Yeah... it's a balancing act there. I think the other body of work for the Standing Orders Committee is going to be looking at how do you deal with 600 amendments being laid on the table of the House during the committee stage. It's a right, it's an entitlement of members of Parliament to do that, and there'd be no desire to cut that off.

But there are, in other parliaments, various provisions in their standing orders to limit how many amendments can be made, et cetera. So there's a need to look at that. I don't think we'll get to that during this parliamentary term. The Standing Orders Committee meets each week of sitting blocks, and has been doing so for quite a while, so a very broad consideration of standing orders.

But this is a very big bit of work, and it sort of comes right up against those constitutional rights as well. Mm-hmm. Yeah. In the Clerk's Office, obviously, your role is the management of legislation and standing orders, and it also is to serve us as MPs.

In terms of, I guess the funding bid you put in and the money you got, is that enough to be able to serve, I don't mean us as individuals, but our role as MPs and politicians and the institution? Yeah. I believe so. What was contained in the Office of Parliament Committee report is what we submitted and asked for, and that's our best estimate of, I think, what we need to deal with the likely volumes, assuming they're similar to how they've been in this parliament.

Yeah. Or they grow a bit. And my final one on this- Can I put the supplement? What changes would we, as members of Parliament, in the way that we work notice? Um, in- In, in, in the changes that you're actually implementing- Yeah... what we will notice I think, one of the key ones might be if you are on a select committee getting submissions, that they, uh, if you think about the principles of the Treaty of Waitangi Bill, where they took months because they all had to be read by individuals.

The aim would be to get them to you much more quickly. But I mean, in, in that case, that took three or four months to get through them all 'cause there were hundreds of thousands of people who had to read them. And what we want to do is develop tools where we can automate some of that process, um, and flag up to human decision-makers those that actually need attention and get the rest of them through to you quickly.

So I think you'll see things coming to you more quickly. Um, uh, and in other areas you should see no reduction in service, for example, in drafting of members' bills, there's a bigger volume of those, um, and dealing with written questions and amendments in committee of the whole house. Really we want to be able to support the growing volumes of them without diminishing the service.

And that's all been driven out of the fact that, um, on that, uh, Treaty Principles Bill, uh, the committee actually had to report before that it had been able to consider all of the submissions. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. So there was a triage of that activity, but it's, um, time is a constraining thing. Practically.

And my final, and this one is around AI and are you using that, and what does it look like in terms of, I guess, protecting, um, not only the information that's put into it- Mm... but also, you know, uh, we're not fully convinced yet of, of what, you know, the future could look in that, and where, where does that fit in the clerk's office in this space?

Well, I mean, we've been using automated speech recognition in Hansard for years. Yeah. And that's really dramatically sped up the, the turnaround of Hansard, which used to take about a, a week, uh, between the member saying something and it being published. Uh, it's now a- available by the next day, in question time within a couple of hours. Um, so, so we've been using it in that way for a long time.

That's meant we've been able to redeploy staff, um, to other work rather than leaving them on transcription. Um, one of the areas we want to explore and, and we've been funded to look at, is using AI, um, as, as I just mentioned to Ms. Lee, to, um, sift through submissions, flag up to a, a human decision maker, ones that, you know, might fall foul of the standing orders. The key thing is that there's always a responsible human being who has to make the decisions, uh, not, not AI. Another useful area might be in written questions.

Um, you know, there's hundreds and thousands of those. You're not allowed to ask the same one multiple times. If when a member's lodging it, they could be told, "This question's just been answered, and here's the answer," that would allow them to ask a different question or already have the information they want. So in areas like that, but we are conscious of the need to have really good data to support it, 'cause it's only as good as the data it can look at.

Um, and, and also, I guess, our responsibility as custodians of that data to be careful with it. So we're not rushing headlong into it, but we do see, you know, value in trying it out. The final area, I think, is, you know, there, there... I think there'll be tools that could be useful for members in doing your own analysis. I don't want to tell you how to do it, um, but I do want to make sure we've got data, um, that's suitable so that you can if you want to use those tools.

Mm. Okay. And Tim has a sup. Yeah, I just wanted to come back, Mr. Speaker. You were talking before about some of the amendments that have been put on the table. So if I think back to the last budget, one was changing the word a person's income, then they said, "Well, there's papers," they changed it to their pay, to their wealth. Uh, one said to change the word income to moolah. But we can't actually honestly argue that that's, there's any democratic intent there. Surely our democracy is more mature than that.

Um, and, and another example might just be in, I don't know, commencement dates changes, something like that, uh, where... And, and I hear your, your, your point that you made was, well, we don't want to take away the constitutional right of members to make amendments, but surely there's a point at which, you know, if we keep the word income as income, do we have to have ridiculous words replacing it and vote on them all individually?

And if it's a, we're in a party vote system here, you know, if you've got 10 different options being put by an, by an opposition party, is that really, you know, if they can't agree on what the, the change should be to, how is Parliament ever gonna agree to that? Is, is there a limit, is, is what I'm getting to. Is there a limit at when it's actually not democratic intent, it's just...

Yeah, I think the, the word that has been used in the past is, is frivolous. Um, but, you know, i- in the end, the clerk's office work, um, according to the standing orders that, that guide them in these things, as well as the conventions of the past. And, uh, there is a, a desire to, um, work out whether or not if a bill has been, uh, uh, and not, uh, let's say, use your example of, um, person's income to, what was the other one you used?

It was something, their, their moolah or whatever. Income. It was change the word income to moolah. Right. Then, then if the intention was to speak that once, then the House has expressed that it does not want to change it, and therefore any other amendment should fall to one side. Those are decisions that need to be supported by, uh, standing orders, and that's what's, you know, being looked at at the present time.

When it comes to, um, your other point, which was about, um, uh, you know, the, does it affect the interior of Parliament, essentially, is what you're saying. I think it does. And so we've just got to proceed carefully with all of this. Yeah. We've got a sup from Lawrence. Thank you. Um, thank you so much, Speaker, and congratulations, Raphael- Thank you... um, on 20 years of service. And I do want to follow up on that particular question because I think it's an important one for, particularly for Office of the Clerk to consider, is we do hear this government,

for example, saying that while previous government did a lot of things under urgency, therefore, we are doing lots of things under urgency. The next government's gonna come in, and then, or next term of Parliament are gonna come in, well, last term of Parliament did all of these. And so we're ever-playing this sort of top trump to try and kind of catch up. And I agree with, I think, Speaker, what you were saying in terms of it may not support in terms of the time that we're spending here, and this was something that was explored in a previous hearing as well.

What sort of-... considerations maybe from through Standing Orders is going to be there around constantly changing. I know Standing Orders was changed to be able to conglomerate and rule out for relevance and et cetera. Is there any further thoughts around changes? Yeah, there needs to be more changes. Parliament doesn't sit. It's that simple.

If you look at the order paper at the moment, the amount of work that's been done by select committees, now sitting there looking for second readings, then you see that the choke point in the legislative process is the parliamentary end of it. Mm. And so, as you will be aware, I've proposed a change to parliamentary sitting hours, which parties I hope are considering.

And that will be something that would come back to Standing Orders to look at. But in essence, you can't have, on the one hand, a capacity to endlessly propose amendments to legislation. But at the same time say, "Well, it's terrible that we're having to take these extra hours to try and achieve things." So it's a bit of a chicken and an egg.

I think Parliament doesn't sit long enough. It's my strong view. And the other thing is, I think Standing Orders do need to change to take out some of that, effectively, amendments that everyone knows is not going to pass but will slow up the process.

And with those changes, let's say we increase the number of sitting hours, is that something that, from a budget perspective, that there is allowances for that extra time that's going to be required? Well, it's likely that if there were to be changes to the sitting hours, they would occur in the next Parliament.

So it would depend entirely on when that next Parliament first sits. And then if there's this necessity for supplementary estimate, the Parliament bill allows for that to occur. Okay, thank you. We've got a primary from Andy. Yeah, two things I wanted to ask about.

First one, Parliament bill or Parliament Act. Just one of the key bits in that was the implementation of increased security arrangements in the precinct, and obviously there's a lot of training and support that was required for that. Just wanted to find out how that was rolling out. Mm. And if all the training's been done and a bit of staff are up to where they need to be and so on.

Thank you. As part of the implementation after the Parliament Act, we needed to run training courses for the existing- Yes... PSOs and then certify those that had passed the course. They did the course in a few tranches because there's quite a few of them.

Yes. And we had most of the PSOs have been currently certified. So the actual arrangement have started. We hoped that we didn't have to use it for years. But within three days we had to use the powers. And I guess that meant that we removed the stage fright of what that would look like. It went well.

And it happened last week or a couple of weeks ago. So it's proven that the training that the PSOs had was successful and they managed those two situations pretty well. Good stuff. And can I ask another one, but it's not related to that particular area, which is about- Is it a quick one?

... precinct. Yeah, it's a quick one. It's just that we had the conversation, I think, last year when you were here as well, about money for the precinct. So we've got money at the moment, which is going into the upgrade projects and new building and so on. But it's the ongoing maintenance thereafter, and I think Treasury had sort of said, "No, we're not really playing ball with that." We've had so much focus on asset management across other areas of what government does, and it felt like Treasury, who should've been one of the organizations who really

pushed for that or should be pushing for that, then was sort of not doing it for Parliament. Well, you have a hopeful- Yeah... hopeful and optimistic view of Treasury and its contributions to the wellbeing of a nation. I don't. I think where we're at at the moment is not an advanced space.

Mm. But there are some big challenges sitting ahead. The Beehive needs a lot of work done on it. And the best way to do that would be to vacate it for about 18 months and let it all be done all at once. So it's a building that was built during the 1970s, opened, I think in 1979. It's had a couple of interior goes at it.

But you've got ventilation systems, you've got outside windows, you've got the structural members on the outside of the building that are all showing significant deterioration. Mm. Mm. And some offices up there do actually have plane intrusion- Yeah. I was told about that, yeah... during bad weather. So it can't be put off forever. But that's a discussion that's ongoing at the moment.

The rest of it, when it comes to the lower library area, that was brought up to code in, I think, 2018. I think I'm right in saying, around that time. But then there was a reconsideration of that code within two years, and it went from being 67% of building code down to 15.

Mm. Now there's been another look at those codes because that seemed like an excessive drop, and the estimates at the moment are that that building will need some work but probably not as much as was initially expected. The challenge is always to make sure that there is confidence among the users about going back into those spaces, and the same would apply to the Press Gallery building above the Beehive Swim Pool, which is in much the same situation. So, a piece of paper came down that said, "This is the new..." Determination

and evacuation was immediately implemented. The question is, was that good information at the time? According to the calculations required at the time, it was, but that engineering is now being challenged significantly with the new approach from the MB people that run this.

So my question really is though, as the custodians of this building, or this collection of buildings, is we ask lots of organizations now, Minister of Infrastructure saying he wants to legislate in that direction as well, say we want asset management plans. It's then there's the resources being put behind those asset management plans, which appear to have been unsupported by the organization which you so dearly love. Are you the Treasury?

Yeah, well, they don't mind waste. Yeah. But we did get some funding for a couple of things. One, to get an asset management system, which we needed desperately. Yeah. So we got the funding for that, which we'll be doing in the next financial year. And we got money at the previous budget, to increase the money we got to fund, to fix different things, so the money that we need to repair the different things we need to repair.

And we got some money as well from the Office of Parliament Committee to just bring us a little bit up to date with contracts that were climbing because of CPI, and increases, but we didn't have that money to cover it. So those cost pressures seem all right. So I think I agree with Mr.

Speaker. I think we are in a much better space now. Okay. Primary from Glee. I want to continue discussion on security. And as the Labor's chief whip, obviously I'm aware of all the things that go on for our caucus, and it's hard to read and see often what is going on. 10.9 million has been allocated, is my understanding.

So firstly, is that for security in general and cybersecurity? But then secondly, in terms of is that enough? Because again, I think we're very fortunate in this country that we do okay, but then I know that we're only one threat or incident away from us losing our innocence. So are you confident that that is enough money to ensure the protection not only of MPs, their families, but also the staff here and in the eco-offices?

Look, it's something that is under constant review and has been the whole time that I've been speaker. And I think the threat levels that come to MPs now are just off the charts, in my opinion. And, as you rightly say, it would just take one person to go from being the champion on the keyboard to the terrorist in real life.

And, so we have good communications with the New Zealand Police, and that's probably now, I think, in a better state than it's been for a time. That's true. They're very, very aware that their role is to... That wasn't him calling or texting .

It was an alert. Thank you. To be readily available, and I think that's a good thing. I think having our own people better trained and with little limited amount of authority to be able to do things is a positive step. Mm-hmm. Sort of ramp the system.

Yes, sir. I think, also, the program that we've got supporting MPs who are out in public. If there's likely to be a choppy situation, then they can get security staff who will turn up. Mm-hmm. And consequent to that, police will be notified.

But it's an unfortunate trend in New Zealand politics. Going into the election, we think that's going to be heightened. Mm-hmm. That threat state. And so, a lot of work's going into that. The police have set up a command unit that's going to look at the, assess the necessity for security around all members of Parliament.

In fact, we've made it clear it's candidates as we go into this coming election. Mm-hmm. Because the way I look at it is if there was just one incident- Yeah... all hell would break loose. There'd be no issue about funding it. And we'd probably go to a far more restricted state, and much more like some other jurisdictions than what we're used to in New Zealand.

So I think we've got to be mindful but careful. Yeah. We've got enough. With the level of services we want to provide now to members, think we are okay. Hopefully we don't need to provide more. But hopefully we'll also get a little bit more uptake in different things, and that's when we have to go back and reassess.

But right now, I think it's fine. Yeah. And I think for all of us sitting on this table, I assume we want to be accessible and want to be able to be out in our communities, so the last thing we want is to... I think that's the beauty of the New Zealand democracy. Mm-hmm. Is cybersecurity part of that? Because obviously that is a key part of it.

Yeah. And my second part, just thanks to your staff, because I know they are doing a lot of work- Yeah... to protect us. So cybersecurity is part of that. Yes, cybersecurity as well. Are you okay? We started a little bit late, and I have one more primary on my list. Are you okay to take that, then we go into the ombudsman part of that? So you're okay to take Lawrence's primary in this part?

Well, let him ask it, then I'll segue right into it. Fair enough. It is quite a quick one. Thank you. Yeah, with that. I'd just like to check, regarding the latest letter that the Audit Office- Sorry, yeah, just keep talking. Sorry, I just- Oh, sorry. Just the latest letter that the Audit Office wrote to Parliamentary Service regarding the use of- Public fund.

And some of the recommendation there, one of the things was around funding for publicity approvals and noting what is parliamentary funded public work- Mm-hmm... and what isn't. Can I just check, in this year's budget, have you given any thoughts to how that could be better managed? Can I?

Yeah. For a start, I think that our report was very favorable to the way in which the publicity assessments are being made. Absolutely. And so that was a tribute to the person who is actually most responsible for that. Yeah. Second, I think, I've had two discussions now with the auditor, trying to narrow down a lot more clearly what is parliamentary purpose. Because it's one of these things that dogs us.

It's very easy for people who have never been in this place, who've never done the job, to say, "Well, this is parliamentary purpose. This isn't parliamentary purpose." The only people who really know what the intention is, is going to be members of Parliament. Mm-hmm. And so, I think we're in a pretty reasonable place with the auditor on that stuff.

And there'll be clear guidelines coming out again for what you can and can't do during the election period, so once Parliament dissolves on the 24th of September or a few days later. But I think that letter was... I interpret it as being more a complimentary letter- Mm-hmm... than a problematic letter.

No, you're absolutely right, Mr. Speaker. Okay. Well, thank you. That brings us to the end of the part with the Office of the Clerk and Parliamentary Services. Now we'll have a swap over to the Ombudsman. Good. I might just adjust my chair, I think. Just...
Thank you. See if that's all right. Welcome. Thank you. So, running five minutes late, but we'll still have our 15 minutes if that's okay.

Was there anything that you wanted to start with, Mr. Speaker, here? No, other than to say, obviously, John was here 12 months ago, I think, at the same meeting, just literally weeks after being appointed into the role. But so far, I think the way in which the Ombudsman's office has continued its work has been without any particular issue at all. And, while I am responsible for their budget, the Ombudsman is an independent officer of Parliament and not

subject to any direction from my office or from anybody else, in fact. It's a very important aspect. I think John's been quite good in making sure that was upheld. So with that, I'm happy to hand over to him for any comment he might like to make. Any comments you'd like to make.

I won't take too much of the committee's time, but thank you for the opportunity to appear. As the speaker says, it's about a year since I've been in the role. The challenges that were put in front of us at the time of my appointment was to improve the timeliness of our complaints process, to ensure that the approach and model that we operated in the office didn't continue to require me to come to Parliament every year and ask for more money as we increased headcount to respond to increasing levels of complaint, to build stronger relationships with the community,

and to improve the impact of the office. Now, that was already building on a very strong position, so I'm not suggesting it was weak, but to think about how we might improve impact. And I'm pleased to be able to say to the committee that I believe we've made progress against all of those metrics. The Ombudsman office, of course, is as important now as it has ever been in enabling trust and confidence in government to be built and sustained.

And the support that we have received from Parliament to allow us to invest in the next financial year in our technology, to allow us to move pay of our people in a way that means it continues to be competitive in the market, enables us to be confident about our ability to meet the expectations of this committee, of Parliament, and of the people of New Zealand into the future.

Thank you. Well, I've got a couple of questions on the list here. So Lawrence, I've got the first one to you. Thank you so much. Thank you for joining us. I know that others have probably questions around the IT update as one of the capacity, but I want to ask the first question is, the concerns that was raised by the subcommittee for OPCAT, for the Operational Protocol to the Convention Against Torture, which has explicitly addressed the fact that the Ombudsman, the funding as a national preventative mechanism is not

ring-fenced. In this year's budget, is there any consideration by the Ombudsman to ensure that there's ring-fenced funding to undertake our obligation on OPCAT? Look, I think that would be a very operational decision and quite specific that would be a bit hard to answer this afternoon. Now, I know you're going to say- We'll take that up... the Ombudsman should be able to- Yes... and if he wants to make a comment, he certainly will.

I'm not here to speak for the Ombudsman. But I think questions like that are sometimes a little difficult to answer in the round. Okay. Did you want to make any further comment? There are different models around the world in relation to the exercise of the OPCAT jurisdiction. A number of ombudsman functions also exercise the NPM function under OPCAT in various parts of the world.

We have a single, what do we call it? Appropriation is the word I was looking for. Yeah Appropriation for the office. You'd think I'd know that when I was coming into this committee, wouldn't you? But there we are. Appropriation for the office. I can assure you that the decisions that are taken and the way in which we organize our work for OPCAT does meet the obligations and the requirements, in our view, of the convention.

But what we see in other jurisdictions is that the personnel that is in charge or involved in making sure that we function as a NPM under OPCAT, that particular role is specifically for the role of our obligations under OPCAT, whereas that role is not specific within the Ombudsman's office.

And so that's what I meant in terms of- Understood... the appropriation, whether there's any consideration to ensure there is a dedicated person as opposed to that person doing multiple other jobs, specifically in terms of obligation Well, in relation to the OPCAT resourcing, those resources are focused on OPCAT.

The challenge of OPCAT in terms of visiting places of detention around the country is a significant challenge, and there isn't capacity for those people to be doing other things. The other point that I'd make is that the New Zealand Parliament has determined the structure of the NPM. They've determined that the Ombudsman should be able to, is a part of that NPM, and they've done that because of not just our capacity in investigating through the OPCAT jurisdiction, but also because of the augmenting strength that the Ombudsman Act gives in that context.

Okay. Thank you. Vanoushi. Thank you, Madam Chair. And firstly, to thank the Ombudsman and your colleagues, and of course, Mr. Speaker, for being with us today, but for the important work that you do in terms of upholding our democracy. I noted the radical increase in terms of demand on service. I think it was 57% up in terms of requests for investigation this year to last. Am I correct that there was also an underspend in the budget in recent years? I'm just trying to understand the increasing pressure with the underspend in the

budget. Yes, there have been underspends in the budget in recent years. We're working hard on making sure that that is not the case as we move forward. We've been investing in technology within our finance function, as you will have seen with the FMIS, the new FMIS system, to better support the office. And as you will have seen in the material, we have a change process going on within the finance function at the moment, which is designed to ensure that we have the capability to enable to support the office as we go through, particularly this,

significant for this office, technology program in the next year. I think we might also mention the balance sheet change, which gave you an increased amount of funding. Sure. As the speaker has just said, it's worth drawing attention to the fact that we've been supported in our ability to do that work, particularly in relation to the wider CRM technology change that we have targeted for next year, next financial year, by the decision to allow us to take money off the balance sheet, which was capital- Yes... and move it to operational expenditure to support and fund that

initiative. So we're very grateful for that. Thank you. And just another budget question. I was a little bit concerned to see the decrease of 1.1 million due to the cessation of temporary funding for international development, and wondered if you could-- It seemed unusual given I understand that it's due to be completed anyway at the end of this budget cycle.

So that's right. So the question was: should we come back to Parliament and ask for a continuation of that funding? In my view, we can manage our activity internationally and support, particularly Pacific Ombudsman's offices, without additional funding. Could I ask where the reductions might happen in terms, because presumably that 1.1 million means that you now need to manage that within operational funding.

Correct. So there's likely less international travel for our people to go into the Pacific and other places, to actually run programs, and more funded travel by partner countries to bring their people to us, to enable us to do that. And there's more online training that we are able to provide, which is obviously less costly to- You'd also be aware that the Tai Kiwa program, Siding Fet, is bringing significant numbers of Pacific- Uh, representatives to New Zealand.

Some of them have been wanting to work with the Ombudsman's office. Some with the Audit Office, and then some Parliamentary Service as well. So, it's not like the connection is completely lost by one budget move. Okay. Lawrence has a supplement.

Just on that, a savings of $1.1 million in just travel alone seems extraordinary. Are there other areas other than travel that's also being saved in, or is that actually just traditionally how much it has cost the Ombudsman's office for? No, no, I'm not saying it would only be in travel.

As I've said, there are other efficiencies if I'm able then to use, as we are, apply some of the capability that might otherwise be applied to supporting other jurisdictions, to supporting the building of our connections with communities across New Zealand, which we are able to do. Then, that is obviously a benefit to the office as well.

So there's a range of benefits in taking a slightly different approach to the delivery of our international services. Right. Thank you. Another primary from Vanushi. I'm thinking about the budgets ahead for the Ombudsman, which I think are extremely important, and the context here is we're seeing two things happening at the same time.

The first is decreasing trust in our public institutions and some might say an avoidance of the OIA process and certainly some news stories recently there. So that's one piece. We then have extra demand being put on the Ombudsman's office, so a significant amount of pressure on the team.

I understand that the minister has asked for an assessment of what the options are that are possible in terms of changing either the scope of the Ombudsman's jurisdiction. I guess my question would be, which way do we go? Do we either go for reform that allows the Ombudsman to do a broader job and try and lift that trust, or do we address the numbers question and draw down the powers of the Ombudsman?

Well, as you'll be-- Sorry. As you'll be aware, the Ombudsman's jurisdictions have grown significantly over relatively recent years, and we've talked about the OPGAT jurisdiction, but there's also the oversight and supervision of Oranga Tamariki. So there are a range of different avenues and issues that we are now having to address within the office. In my view, we have the resources to be able to address those issues.

If you're asking me whether I would advocate for an expansion of our jurisdictions, I don't think I would. And the reason that I don't think I would is that I believe that the combination of the OIA and the OA and the PDA, which is the Protective Disclosures legislation, all of which we are able to draw on, give us a very powerful mechanism or set of mechanisms to be able to address the challenges that are in front of us and to build trust in the way that you've described.

Thank you. I think specifically for me, there remains a question about penalties and what we do when we get to the point where we've seen a breach, and yet it seems to persist, and should we see the trust continue to decline, then that might be something we want to address.

And the other proposal which I'm quite in favor of is looking at what happens when the Ombudsman makes a direction and nothing is done. So in other jurisdictions, you would be aware that there's the ability for those to be returned to Parliament. And I think about decisions such as the PERU, so the Persons of Extreme Risk Unit, very serious obviously, and what might be more suitable or appropriate in those circumstances.

Well, the reality is that on very few occasions are recommendations that are made by the Ombudsman not taken up by the agencies to which those recommendations are made. You've talked about PERU, which is one in which there was a recommendation in relation to the PERD, the directorate itself, in which Sir Peter suggested that it be disestablished.

That hasn't been taken up. That is true. But even in relation to that report, there were significant recommendations in relation to contact between people, around time in cells, around the way in which that mechanism operated which have been worked on by corrections and that has made progress. In relation specifically to the question of penalties, while I recognize that it is tempting to think that you put penalties in place, and I know that some of the Australian jurisdictions, particularly in the OIA space, have penalties in

place. In fact, the most powerful mechanism, in my view, to get agencies to engage with and comply with our recommendations is in fact the Public Service Commission and the fact that the targets set by the Public Service Commission for individual CEs in relation to compliance and meeting the requirements of the OI legislation.

That compliance, as you know, because it's publicly disclosed, runs at fairly strong levels. So while there is pressure within the system at the moment, particularly resourcing pressure in some OI teams , there is still, in my view, a significant focus And genuine focus on endeavoring to comply with the legislation.

That doesn't mean there isn't some gaming from time to time, and certainly we call that out. But I don't believe that turning the ombudsman function from an independent, free, and basically non-judicial in terms of being able to impose penalties, into something closer to a judicial function is a good idea. I think it would actually make it less attractive to many complainants who come to us because of the characteristics of the jurisdiction at the moment.

I have three more supps from people, and that's going to bring it to the end at that point. So Vanushi, Lawrence, and then Melissa. Thank you very much. Just going back to your first response. To confirm, I appreciate that you acknowledge you can cope with the burden of the current caseload under the current budget, but has the minister instructed or sought information about data and the rising costs and the burden?

I'm not sure which minister you're talking about. I'm sorry, Minister Goldsmith. It's an independent agency. But he can still request information about the- Not that I-- certainly not to me directly. If he's been in touch with the office, I'm surprised I wouldn't know. But no, I don't think so. Thank you. Thank you. Lawrence? Yeah. Thank you. I just want to follow up on what you said before and what Vanushi Walters has asked. I do want to check then, in order to maintain public trust of our public service, and we know there's a couple of investigations, Te Whatu Ora being another

one, what then is the accountability mechanism and what are some of the mechanisms for deterrence? Because if it's just recommendation, then how do we know that the same happen with emergencies? Well, often our penalty would be electoral failure, wouldn't it? I think one of the problems that clearly the ministry has, ministries have recent times where something might be under consideration by a government or by a minister to even take to a government.

And that requires a degree of it will take time. It will mean that you wouldn't necessarily want to have that or at all have that as a public discussion because it's a policy matter. So I've observed in the House over the last parliament, and this one, situations where ministers have been, I think, quite genuinely endeavoring to reach a policy position, but then get tripped up because official information comes out that's got advice that might be contrary to the position that a government ultimately took. Now, we're not governed by the

people who give us that advice. We're governed by the people who are elected to make decisions on the advice. So I can think of two situations where I won't go into, one in the last government and one now, where ministers were expected to explain why they hadn't done something that officials recommended.

Now, that's the prerogative of the government, and I think you've got to be very careful about saying we've got to have a whole pile of penalties if you don't comply in a timely fashion, et cetera, to the OIA requirements. Okay, and thank you. Last question from Melissa.

Thank you very much. I wanted to go and talk about the factors in terms of fact, trust. And previously when you were here, we were actually talking about how often for the ethnic communities and the minorities don't even understand the concept of what an ombudsman is. And one of the things that we discussed was potentially reaching out from the Ombudsman's office.

I see that you've been reaching out to the ethnic communities. So what is that all about? Well, it's all about doing exactly what you've described, which is about ensuring that all communities in this country know about the office, understand the jurisdictions of the office, and understand that they can come to the office to support them if they have issues with elements of the Crown.

Sure. Framed it as how were you received and how did they react to the information? Yeah. So, I think if you go into a room generally with various of our ethnic communities, and you, say, ask the question, how many of you know what the ombudsman is and what they do- Like-... you'll get a very, very, very small showing of hands.

But there is real interest once you explain what the ombudsman does. And obviously, we have materials that are available to support that advocacy. We've had a very good engagement with the Asian Family Services group up in Auckland recently, who are doing some very, very interesting social services work, and we're very keen to align and engage, and similarly with Iwi Māori, similarly with the- Pasifika... Pasifika communities around the country.
Well, that brings us to the end, and thank you very much, both of you, for your engagement with us this afternoon. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. See you later.
